title: Azure App Service vs AWS App Runner for Small Platform Teams
excerpt: DEV Wagtail seed article for azure app service vs aws app runner for small platform teams in the cloud-aws-azure category.

value.title: Azure App Service vs AWS App Runner for Small Platform Teams

body.value:
This seeded article belongs to the cloud-aws-azure category and is meant to populate the Wagtail DEV blog with realistic technical content. It follows the same StreamField structure as the existing sample data, so the seeder can call to_python(...) and validate the payload before saving the page.

The goal is not only to test page creation, but also to exercise sidebar category filtering, tag chips, publish timestamps, and rich article rendering under /articles/. Keeping three posts per category makes the local dataset feel balanced and makes sorting by sort_order easy to verify.

value.title: Why this helps locally
value.text: A seeded technical post like this proves that category filters, tag relationships, and body block rendering all work together.

body.value:

The goal is not only to test page creation, but also to exercise sidebar category filtering, tag chips, publish timestamps, and rich article rendering under /articles/. Keeping three posts per category makes the local dataset feel balanced and makes sorting by sort_order easy to verify.

value.title: Operational reminder
value.text: Keep the JSON shape stable. Each post should provide slug, title, excerpt, author, category, tags, publish status, publish time, and a StreamField body array.

value.caption: Representative snippet
value.code: Use managed identity, secret store integration, and structured logs as the default baseline.

value.caption: Simple flow
value.diagram:
flowchart TD
    A[Seed JSON] --> B[Read file]
    B --> C[Validate StreamField blocks]
    C --> D[Create or update BlogPostPage]
    D --> E[Publish revision]

value.title: Design takeaway
value.text: The most useful DEV content is content that looks real enough to validate templates, but simple enough to maintain as source-controlled seed data.
